flashcards.Back: An internal data file stored in a router's RAM that contains a list of known network paths. The router uses this information to determine where to forward packets.
A single-letter code (e.g., C, S, O, D) at the beginning of a routing table entry that identifies the source of the routing information.
The network or subnet portion of an IP address. in a routing table, it represents the destination network ID.
The value that defines the size of the destination network. it is typically shown in CIDR notation (e.g., /24) in modern routing tables.
The IP address of the adjacent router interface to which a packet should be sent to continue its journey toward the final destination.
The physical or logical port on the local router through which a packet exits to reach the next hop or the final destination network.
A value from 0 to 255 used to rate the reliability of a routing source. if multiple sources offer a route to the same network, the one with the lowest AD is installed.
A numerical value used by a specific routing protocol to rank multiple paths to the same destination. The path with the lowest metric is chosen as the best route.
The default route used by a router when no specific entry in the routing table matches a packet's destination IP address. it is denoted by an asterisk (*).
A network that is physically attached to one of the router's active interfaces. it has an administrative distance of 0.
A /32 (IPv4) or /128 (IPv6) route for the specific IP address assigned to a router interface. it allows the router to process packets destined for itself efficiently.
A manual routing entry configured by an administrator. it has a default administrative distance of 1 and is highly reliable but not dynamic.
A route learned dynamically via the Open Shortest Path First protocol. it has a default administrative distance of 110.
A route learned via Cisco's Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol. it has a default internal administrative distance of 90.
A route learned via the Routing Information Protocol. it uses hop count as a metric and has a default administrative distance of 120.
An EIGRP route that was redistributed from a different routing source. it has a higher administrative distance of 170.
The administrative distance for External Border Gateway Protocol routes, which is 20. These are highly trusted as they come from outside the autonomous system.
The metric used by RIP, representing the number of routers a packet must cross to reach its destination. The maximum limit is 15 hops.
The metric used by OSPF, based on the bandwidth of the links along the path. lower cost indicates a faster and preferred route.
The metric calculation used by EIGRP, which by default combines Bandwidth and Delay to determine the best path.
A backup static route configured with a higher administrative distance than the primary route. it only enters the routing table if the primary path fails.
The primary Cisco IOS command used to view the IPv4 routing table and its components.
The state where all routers in a network have finished exchanging information and their routing tables are consistent and accurate.
The process where a router must look up a next-hop IP in the routing table to find the corresponding outgoing interface.
The introductory text in the 'show ip route' output that explains what each letter code (C, S, O, etc.) stands for.
A route that has been successfully added to the active routing table because it is the most trustworthy path to a destination.
A special route that matches all destination IP addresses. it is used to send traffic to the internet or a central hub when no specific route exists.
The concept that static routes take precedence over dynamic routes because they have a lower administrative distance (1 vs 90/110/120).
The two-step logical process a router uses: 1. Compare AD between different sources. 2. Compare metrics within the same source.
A timer in some routing table entries showing how long it has been since the router last received a valid update for that path.
